coredata.dc:title: Microwave irradiation and color converting film application of carbon dots originated from wasted toner powder
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Woo Tae
Moon, Byung Kee
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: Over the past few decades, sustainable precursor of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been researched due to their environmental benefits, cheap and abundant resource. Among various precursors, waste toner powder is attractive because of its nitrogen-containing organic composition, ever-growing quantity, high toxicity, and low recycling. In this work, we synthesized fluorescent CDs through the microwave irradiation of waste toner powder. The CDs exhibited hydrophilic characteristics and an amorphous structure owing to the microwave irradiation of the waste toner powder in the ethanol solvent. The CDs exhibited dominant yellow emission of 557 nm under 300 nm excitation due to the presence of nitrogen atoms as dopants. To investigate the performance of CDs in LED applications, yellow LEDs were fabricated by combining UV LEDs and CDs. The fabricated LEDs emitted yellow light with (0.411, 0.461) of CIE 1931 coordinates. These results indicate that CDs are favorable luminescent materials for LED applications.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Development of the CD coated-glass and its LED application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Normal and high-resolution (inset) TEM images and (b) particle size distribution of CDs, red line is Gaussian fitting of the particle size distribution.
Comparison of FT-IR spectra between wasted toner powder and CDs.
(a) Full range and high resolution XPS spectra of the CDs of (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) chlorine, (e) sodium, and (f) oxygen.
Excitation dependent PL spectra of the CDs.
(a) EL spectra and (b) CIE color coordinates of the fabricated LEDs under VF = 5.2 V, IF = 20 mA.

head.ce:title: Microwave irradiation and color converting film application of carbon dots originated from wasted toner powder

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine-Bionics convergence technology center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aMarine-Bionics convergence technology center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the past few decades, sustainable precursor of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been researched due to their environmental benefits, cheap and abundant resource. Among various precursors, waste toner powder is attractive because of its nitrogen-containing organic composition, ever-growing quantity, high toxicity, and low recycling. In this work, we synthesized fluorescent CDs through the microwave irradiation of waste toner powder. The CDs exhibited hydrophilic characteristics and an amorphous structure owing to the microwave irradiation of the waste toner powder in the ethanol solvent. The CDs exhibited dominant yellow emission of 557 nm under 300 nm excitation due to the presence of nitrogen atoms as dopants. To investigate the performance of CDs in LED applications, yellow LEDs were fabricated by combining UV LEDs and CDs. The fabricated LEDs emitted yellow light with (0.411, 0.461) of CIE 1931 coordinates. These results indicate that CDs are favorable luminescent materials for LED applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention in various fields, including solar cells, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), catalysis, sensors, and bio-imaging applications, owing to their tunable optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, high quantum yields, high stability, abundant carbon sources, and noble functionalization [1–13]. To improve and control the luminescent properties of CDs, previous research has reported numerous synthetic strategies using various carbon sources, surface functionalization, purification methods, synthesis methods, and their conditions [14–36]. Among these synthetic strategies, selecting a suitable carbon source is significant to synthesize the CDs as their chemical composition and structure affect the structural and luminescent properties of CDs. In addition, considering the synthesis cost, resource saving, and environmental effects, recycling waste organic materials as the carbon source for CDs is an advantage. Previous studies have developed CD synthesis methods with various carbon sources, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, citric acid, sucrose, waste tires, chewing gum, and frying oil [37–43].

In particular, waste electrical and electronic equipment (WEEE), generated when the equipment reaches the end of life, is one of the fastest growing wastes in the world [44]. Rapid progress in technology and mass production have caused regressed products with older technologies to become obsolete more rapidly, and their quantity has also increased. Most studies on WEEE recycling focus on computers, TVs, air conditioners, washing machines, and refrigerators. Nevertheless, a few waste toner cartridge recycling methods have been reported. In general, WEEE, including waste toner cartridges, is disposed of through landfilling or incineration [45]. This disposal causes critical damage to the environment due to the contamination of soil and the release of toxic gasses, which are caused by toxic chemicals, especially from toner powder. Waste toner powder, a type of electronic waste, is generated as a result of malfunction and renovation of laser printers and duplicators. Although waste toner powder represents 8% of the waste toner cartridge weight, the organic compound is toxic and difficult to decompose in human body and soil [46,47]. Thus, recycling waste toners is crucial for environmental protection. These waste toner powders can be reused as fillers in synthetic rubber manufacturing, for iron oxide transformation, as a source of graphene oxide hydrogel, and through utilization in asphalt cement [48–50]. Although these recycling methods have been developed, tons of waste toner powder are continuously generated and dumped into the soil.

According to previous patents on toner powder, waste toner powder contains various organic compounds, such as organic dyes (diketopyrrolopyrrole, quinacridone, condensed azo-compounds, etc.), dispersing agents (styrene-acrylate, polyester, and methacrylate-based resin), and settlement resins (ester wax, paraffin wax, amid wax, etc.) [51]. These organic compounds are nitrogen-containing carbonaceous materials that possess amide, urethane, urea, and azo bonding [52]. From the CD synthesis point of view, the application of a nitrogen-containing carbonaceous material as a carbon source for CDs is useful for synthesizing heteroatom-doped CDs. Compared to intrinsic CDs, heteroatom-doped CDs exhibit enhanced luminescence quantum yields and tunable luminescence wavelengths. Zhang et al. studied red-emission CDs derived from p-phenylenediamine [53]. The luminescence quantum yield of CDs reached 41.1% at 608 nm emission. In the study by Wang, sulfur-doped CDs were prepared through the hydrothermal processing of durian [24]. The sulfur-doped CDs exhibited 600 nm light emission under 477 nm excitation and 79% luminescence quantum yield. Tian's research reported the yellow emission of nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs derived from 3-aminophenylboronic acid [54]. The CDs exhibited dominant 540 nm emission under 490 nm excitation. The luminescence quantum yield of the CDs was 23.8%. Thus, using waste toner powder as the starting material has the advantages of enhancing the luminescent properties of CDs and recycling WEEE.

In this study, we synthesized luminescent CDs through the microwave irradiation of waste toner powder as the starting material. We analyzed the structural and morphological properties of the prepared CDs. The absorption spectra of the CDs were in the range of UV from visible light. The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs displayed dominant yellow emission at 557 nm (under 300 nm excitation) and excitation-independent emission characteristics. Based on these properties, the CDs were applied as the remote phosphor in the LED application. The electroluminescence performance of the fabricated LEDs was analyzed by measuring their electroluminescence spectra.

Red toner powder was obtained by disassembling a waste toner cartridge (CLT-M504.S; Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd.). Ethanol and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. A microwave oven (700 W, EKRL150DWK, Winia Electronics) was used as a microwave reactor. The UV-LED (λex = 310 nm, VF = 5.2 V, and IF = 20 mA) was procured from Cree, USA.

The CDs were synthesized using a microwave irradiation method, where 0.3 g of red toner powder was dispersed in 10 mL of ethanol. The dispersed solution was poured in a polypropylene bottle and the cap of the bottle was closed. The microwave irradiation of the dispersed solution was achieved through heating in a microwave oven for 30 s at a power of 350 W. To separate the large particles in the solution product, the solution was purified through sonication, centrifugation, and membrane filtration. To extract the CDs from the purified solution, dialysis was performed against ethanol with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the CD coated-glass and its LED application
ce:section.ce:para: The CD film was fabricated using a solution casting method. PVP was dispersed in the CD solution. The PVP-CD film solution was then dropped onto a polypropylene film. To eliminate small bubbles in the film, the PVP-CD films were dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 2 h. The CD film was separated by bending the polypropylene film.

The morphology of the CDs was observed using a field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F) with a copper grid. The Raman spectra of the CDs were recorded using a Raman spectrometer (JASCO, NRS-5100) with a 532 nm laser. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using an FT-IR spectrometer (JASCO, FT/IR-6700). The optical absorption properties were obtained using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (JASCO, V-670). The photoluminescence spectra and quantum yield of the CDs were obtained using a time-resolved spectrometer (JASCO, FP-8500) with an integrating sphere (JASCO, ILF-835). Spectral correction of the spectrometer was performed using rhodamine B in ethylene glycol. The internal (εin) and external (εex) efficiencies (Photoluminescence quantum yield) of the samples were calculated as follows:
ε
i n
=
E 2
L 1
,
ε
e x
=
E 2
L 1
−
L 2
where L1 and L2 are the integration of the excitation light scattered by the inner surface of the integrating sphere without and with the CD solution, respectively, and E2 is the integration of the sample emission.

Fig. 1 shows the (a) TEM images and (b) size distribution of the CDs. The CDs exhibited a spherical shape with high dispersity. The lattice of the CDs was not detected, indicating that the CDs had an amorphous structure. The Gaussian fitting of their size distribution indicated that the average diameter of the CDs was approximately 2.1 nm.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of the pristine toner powder and CDs. The FT-IR spectra of the CDs exhibited weak absorption peaks at 3440 and 3270 cm−1, however these peaks were not detected in the FT-IR spectra of the waste toner powder. This difference originated from the surface passivation of the CDs in the ethanol solution. Characteristic absorption peaks were detected at approximately 2921 cm−1 in both samples and were related to the stretching vibration of the –CH bonds. The existence of the C = O bonds in both samples was analyzed by detecting the characteristic absorption at 1721 cm−1 [55]. The absorption peaks at approximately 1606 cm−1 were due to the overlap of the characteristic absorptions by the in-plane stretching vibration of the aromatic carbon [56]. The absorptions between 1505 and 1445 cm−1 were also due to C–C stretching vibrations in the aromatic ring and azo bonding [56, 57]. The absorption peaks between 1403 and 1341 cm−1 originated from the bending vibrations of the aliphatic –CH3, CNH, and–OH bonds [58]. The absorption peak at 1243 cm−1 emerged from the stretching vibration of the CO bonds. The stretching vibrations of CO-C and COH were revealed by the absorption peaks at 1124 and 1100 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 1017 cm−1 originated from the in-plane vibration of the –CH bonds. The characteristic peak at 829 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of the mono-substituted CO-C bonds. In the FT-IR spectra of the waste toner powder, the absorption peaks at 750 and 698 cm−1 were due to the out-of-plane vibration of –CH. In the FT-IR spectra of the CDs, a characteristic absorption at 729 nm was confirmed, which was due to the stretching vibration of the aliphatic –CH2 bonds [59]. Compared with the FT-IR spectra of the toner powder, the characteristic absorption of the organic bonds in the CDs increased, except for the absorption peaks of the –CH bonds. In particular, the absorption of the CO and CO-C bonds in the CDs increased [60]. These results indicate that microwave irradiation forms various organic bonds in the CDs through the polymerization and condensation of the –CH bonds and condensation of various organic compounds in the toner powder, such as polymer and dye molecules. During a purification process, the particle which size was over 10 nm, is separated and filtered by ultrasonication, vacuum filtration, and dialysis process. These possible formation mechanisms of the CDs were depicted in Fig. 3 .

Based on the toner powder patent, the XPS spectra of the CDs were analyzed to investigate the chemical bonding of the CDs. According to the patent, the toner is chemically composed of carbon, nitrogen, chlorine, sodium, and oxygen. Fig. 4 shows the (a) full-range and high-resolution XPS spectra of the CDs of (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) chlorine, (e) sodium, and (f) oxygen. The full range of the XPS spectra shows that carbon, nitrogen, chlorine, sodium, fluorine, and oxygen atoms existed in the CDs. The atomic ratios of carbon, nitrogen, chlorine, sodium, and oxygen were calculated to be 67.2, 3.2, 0.4, 0.4, and 28.9%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the CC/C = C, CN/C = N, C = O, and OC = O bonds were detected at 285.0, 286.0, 286.7, and 289.1 eV, respectively [61,62]. The existence of the CC/C = C bonds in the CDs was the result of the polymerization of the organic (binder and dye) composite by the microwave irradiation. The pyrrolic CN/C = N bonds of the CDs originated from the azo-bonding of the dye composition [63]. The C = O and OC = O bonds in the CDs were due to the polymerization of the organic composition and oxidation in the ethanol solvent. Fig. 4(c) shows that the nitrogen bonds in the CDs existed prior to the pyrrolic carbon-nitrogen bonding. During the microwave irradiation process, the azo bonds of the dye could be broken and polymerized to form the pyrrolic bonds. Fig. 4(d) shows the existence of the Cl2p3/2 and Cl2p1/2 bonds at 199.1 and 200.7 eV, respectively [64]. Fig. 4(e) depicts the existence of Na1s bonds, which originated from sodium sulfonate in the dye composition. Fig. 4(f) shows that the C = O, CO/COH, and –COOH bonds existed owing to the polymerization and oxidation during the microwave irradiation process [65–67].

To compare wasted toner powder and carbon dots, the XPS spectra of both materials were measured. Figure S1 shows (a) full range and high resolution XPS spectra of the wasted toner powder of (b) carbon, (c) nitrogen, (d) chlorine, (e) sodium, and (f) oxygen. In full scan spectra, nitrogen atoms were existed in carbon dots, while nitrogen atoms were not detected in the wasted toner powder. The atomic ratio of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, chlorine, and sodium was 51.6, 0.9, 47.0, 0.3, and 0.3%, respectively. We assume that the quantity of nitrogen was increased by microwave irradiation. During a microwave irradiation process, the volatile organics can be evaporated by polymerization and condensation reaction. In order to compare the ratio of organic bondings, the ratio of bonding ‘x’ (Rx) was calculated to be
R x
=
A x
∑ A
where ΣA is integration of all of bondings in C1s spectra, and ax is integration of bonding ‘x’. The calculated organic bonding ratio of carbon dots and wasted toner powder were shown in Table S1. The ratio of CC bondings were decreased after microwave irradiation, while ratios of CN and C = O bondings were increased. The N1s, Cl2p and Na1s spectra of wasted toner powder was not detected, since the quantity of nitrogen, chlorine and sodium atoms is too low to detect them.

Fig. 5 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs. The absorption peak at 242 nm was correlated with the π-π* transition of graphite. Chromophoric absorption peaks were detected at 284 nm, and the peaks at 298 nm were related to the existence of organic dyes on the surface of the CDs. The absorption peak at 317 nm was due to the n-π* transition of the C = O and CN bonds in the CDs. The absorption band centered at 527 nm originated from the azo bonds in the CDs. These azo bonds originated from the pristine toner powder [68].

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectra of the CDs. The characteristic D, D’, and G bands were detected at 1361 and 1599 cm−1. The D and D’ bands originated from the in-plane longitudinal and transverse optical absorption at the edge distortion and structural defect in the graphene structure [69]. The G band is related to the sp2 carbon structure of graphene or the benzene ring structure [69]. In addition, an I band, which is related to the CC, C = C, or sp2-sp3 carbon bonding, was observed at 1177 cm−1 [69]. The peaks at 1264 and 1255 cm−1 corresponded to the I* band, which was the Raman scattering of nitrogen doping in the CDs [69]. Compared with the peak intensities and noise signals, the intensity of these bands was low, indicating that the CDs possessed an amorphous structure. The synthetic energy was insufficient to form a 2D structure with a high crystallinity.

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated further. Fig. 7 shows the excitation-dependent PL spectra of the CDs. The emission spectra show that the CDs exhibited two excitation bands in the UV and blue-green regions. Compared with the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra, the UV excitation band was generated by the C = O, C = N, and chromophoric bonds of the CDs. The excitation band in the blue-green region originated from the azo bonding of the CDs. In addition, the CDs exhibited dominant yellow emission at 557 nm as they were excited by 300 nm light. In addition, the CDs exhibited a weak blue emission peak at 438 nm under the UV excitation. The yellow and blue emissions of the CDs exhibited excitation-independent emission characteristics; the excited electrons were generated by the C = O bonds, C = N bonds, chromophoric states, and azo bonds of the CDs.

According to previous studies, CDs based on waste organic composition and natural composition exhibit dominant blue emission under UV excitation and excitation-dependent emission properties. Few papers have reported excitation-independent emissions and longer-wavelength emissions as UV-excitation behaviors of CDs, which are based on waste or natural organic composition [24]. Based on previous studies, the waste or natural organic compounds were based on a low nitrogen-containing composition, such as cellulose, glucose, and sucrose [26,70-73]. Compared with the aforementioned composition, the waste toner powder was transformed to a nitrogen-containing organic composition, such as the azo-dye and nitrogen-containing binder. In addition, the binding structure of the carbon-nitrogen atoms is an important factor in determining the bandgap in CDs. The binding structure of the carbon-nitrogen atoms can be changed into pyrrolic, pyridinic, and graphitic structures [11,74-76]. Yan et al. reported the synthesis and application of the fluorometric DNA hybridization assay of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) derived from waste toner [77]. The GOQDs exhibited dominant emission at 445 nm under 340 nm light excitation. In addition, GOQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emissions. The model of the toner cartridge and the color of the waste toner were not included in Yan's report.

In the XPS spectra of CDs, the carbon-nitrogen bondings were detected in C1s and N1s spectra of carbon dots (Fig. 4(b) and (c)), while carbon-nitrogen bondings were not detected in the XPS spectra of wasted toner powder (Figure S1(b) and (c)). Also, in Fig. 4(c), the bonding structure of carbon-nitrogen bonding was pyrrolic structure. Wang's paper show that the high pyrrolic nitrogen and amino nitrogen makes wider solvatochromic shifts and higher quantum yields [78]. According to past studies, the pyrrolic carbon-nitrogen bonding affect to the CDs to decrease bandgap energy, increase electron density, improve localization of electronic states, and promote charge transfer process [79–83]. For these reasons, we assumed that these phenomena originated from nitrogen doping and the pyrrolic binding structure of carbon-nitrogen atoms in the CDs.

The LED is the largest application field for phosphors. In LED applications, phosphors are applied as color-conversion materials to realize their objective color. Compared with conventional phosphor materials, such as inorganic phosphors, chalcogenide-based quantum dots, and organic materials, CDs have advantages such as low toxicity, abundant resources, high photo-stability, small size, and easy and low-cost synthesis. In this study, the CDs were useful because of their dominant yellow light emission and excitation-independent emission. To demonstrate the LED applications of the CDs, a luminescent film was fabricated by mixing the CD solution and PVP. The LEDs were fabricated by combining a 310 nm LED and the fabricated film. Fig. 8 depicts (a) electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the fabricated spectra and (b) their CIE 1931 color coordinates. The electroluminescence spectra of the fabricated LEDs exhibited broad emission between 320 and 500 nm and two sharp emission peaks at 540 and 580 nm. The CIE 1931 coordinates of the electroluminescence spectra were calculated to be (0.371, 0.479). The internal and external quantum efficiencies of the CDs were calculated to be 9.2 and 8.4%, respectively. To explore the stability of the fabricated LEDs, the EL spectra was measured for various applied voltages. Figure S2 shows voltage-dependent EL spectra of the fabricated LEDs. The intensity of EL spectra increased, while the emission spectra of CDs film was not shifted by raising applied voltages. This result indicates that the fabricated LEDs shows high electrical and thermal stability.

CDs were synthesized through the microwave irradiation of waste toner powder. The morphological and structural characteristics of the CDs were analyzed. The CDs exhibited an average diameter of 2.1 nm and amorphous structure. A comparison between the FT-IR spectra of the CDs and waste toner powder showed that the polymerization and condensation reactions were caused by the microwave irradiation. The CDs exhibited yellow emission bands centered at 557 nm under 300 nm excitation. Compared with inorganic phosphors and chalcogenide quantum dots, the CDs had advantages such as low toxicity, abundant resources, small size, good photo-stability, and easy and low-cost synthesis. Based on these advantages and characteristics, a luminescent film was fabricated by combining the PVP and CD solutions. Remote LEDs were realized by combining the luminescent film and UV LEDs. The electroluminescence spectra of the realized LEDs were calculated to be (0.371, 0.479). These results indicate that CDs can be favorable yellow-emitting phosphors in UV LED-based white LEDs.

Woo Tae Hong: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization. Byung Kee Moon: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Hyun Kyoung Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF- 2016R1D1A1B01014204, 2020R1A2C400219311, 2021R1A6A3A01088346).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2022.111999.
